prompt: hey i wnat to know what alla re the existing slutions fr skin disease detection using cnn,i mean the projects for cnn models focusing on some specific diseases
response: Existing CNN-based skin disease detection projects typically focus on: melanoma/skin cancer classification (using datasets like ISIC or HAM10000), psoriasis detection, eczema classification, and general dermatological lesion classification (benign vs malignant). Common architectures used include ResNet, EfficientNet, and VGG variants, often fine-tuned via transfer learning on dermoscopic images. Most published solutions combine CNN feature extraction with data augmentation to handle limited medical image datasets.

prompt: I have done some working on performing or choosing of many projects in the healthcare domains, which adds a lot of weight to the resume, and we want to find which actually works in the healthcare domain that stands out in the resume, and which companies are actually looking for healthcare projects that can help in having these kind of projects can help in easy retrieval for those companies. Could you please suggest a few of the projects and the companies which can be associated who want to be standout? The level can be intermediate to advanced, tech stack can be used in Python language. Yeah.
response: Strong standout healthcare projects (Python-based, intermediate-advanced): 1) Medical image classification/segmentation (X-ray, MRI, CT scans using CNNs). 2) Disease risk prediction models from EHR/clinical data. 3) Clinical NLP - extracting insights from doctor's notes or symptom text. 4) Drug interaction/side-effect prediction systems. 5) Patient readmission risk models. Companies actively hiring for healthcare AI include GE Healthcare, Philips, Siemens Healthineers, Optum/UnitedHealth, Innovaccer, and health-focused startups - highlighting a project with real clinical relevance and proper evaluation metrics (not just accuracy) stands out most.

prompt: now we are actually going to work with a mini project. We want to check out what are the papers required, so what is it as air quality index reduction and air quality index predictor based on the air quality index criteria based on the data, what are the heat map insights required for this particular area. So and any, if possible, any reduction techniques so that we can reduce the air quality index and any changes. So we want to make a particular mini project, so I want you to give me a detailed analysis of the pros, cons, and what is the scope of the project? Are there any existing solutions for this? What all are the resources required for this particular project? And I would like you to give me a realistic, typical plan within four weeks to complete the project. So the tech stack will be used in this obviously Python as we're using AI and ML integrations with even in ML integrations, we will be taking only numerical datasets. And also I would like to know what all are the datasets are there, what is the entire, what is the entire, planning steps involved to build this project successfully? And what is the scope, which of the SDGs goals it aligns to, and in which case, and what is the probability that this project stands out and what all we have to do to make this project stand out?
response: For an AQI Prediction + Reduction Insight project: Pros - real-world relevance, plentiful open datasets, aligns with SDG 3 (Health) and SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities). Cons - purely numerical AQI prediction is a well-covered problem so differentiation matters; reduction "techniques" are policy-level, not something a model can directly execute. Datasets: CPCB India, US EPA AirData, Kaggle AQI datasets. 4-week plan: Week 1 - data collection/cleaning + EDA with heatmaps of pollutant correlation. Week 2 - build regression/time-series models (Random Forest, XGBoost, or LSTM) to predict AQI. Week 3 - build a dashboard showing predictions + pollutant contribution analysis. Week 4 - write up reduction recommendations based on which pollutants/factors your model shows as most impactful, plus documentation. To stand out, add a geospatial heatmap of pollution hotspots and tie recommendations to specific data-driven insights rather than generic advice.

prompt: I'm a little beginner of technicals, so I would like you to give me some basics content on. I would like to know first exactly what does backend mean? How is it helping out? How backend helps in creating web? What is the role of backend? I mean, front-end is used for visualization. How exactly backend is? And what is the purpose of database? How database helps in fetching? You know, this whole SD, so much, frontend, backend, stack, all these words are fancy words. I don't exactly know what it does. As I get the breakdown each of the words, I can try helping out myself. Help me out this.
response: Frontend is everything the user sees and interacts with (buttons, layouts, forms) - it runs in the browser. Backend is the behind-the-scenes logic that processes requests, handles business rules, and talks to the database - it runs on a server, invisible to the user. The database stores and organizes data persistently (users, orders, posts), and the backend fetches/writes data from it when the frontend asks. A "stack" is just the combination of technologies used (e.g., frontend framework + backend language + database) to build the whole app together.

prompt: how to build it sing llm gimme structured prompt
response: A solid structured prompt template for building with an LLM: "Role: [who the AI should act as]. Context: [relevant background info]. Task: [exactly what you want done]. Constraints: [format, length, tone, things to avoid]. Output format: [how you want the response structured]." Filling in each section explicitly reduces ambiguity and gets much more consistent results than a freeform question.

prompt: I'm a final year AI & ML student. Create a complete roadmap from where I am today to becoming an AI Engineer. Tell me what to learn, what to skip, projects to build and an estimated timeline.
response: 3-month roadmap: Month 1 - Solidify Python, math foundations (linear algebra, stats), and core ML (scikit-learn, classic algorithms). Skip deep theoretical proofs unless research-bound. Build a classic ML project (e.g., tabular prediction). Month 2 - Deep learning fundamentals (PyTorch/TensorFlow), CNNs/transformers basics, and one deep learning project (image/NLP). Month 3 - LLM/GenAI tooling (prompt engineering, RAG, vector DBs, LangChain), deploy one end-to-end AI application with an API and simple frontend, and start interview prep (DSA + ML system design). Skip chasing every new framework - depth in fundamentals + 2-3 solid deployed projects beats breadth.

prompt: Explain Agentic AI from absolute scratch. Assume I know nothing. Cover memory, planning, reasoning, tool calling, MCP, LangGraph, LangChain, CrewAI and how everything fits together.
response: Agentic AI means an LLM doesn't just answer once - it can plan multi-step actions, use tools (like search or code execution), remember context across steps, and decide what to do next based on results. Memory lets it retain relevant info across a task. Planning breaks a goal into steps. Reasoning is the model "thinking through" what to do. Tool calling lets it invoke external functions (APIs, calculators, search). MCP (Model Context Protocol) standardizes how models connect to tools/data sources. LangChain and LangGraph are frameworks for building these multi-step agent workflows in code; CrewAI focuses on orchestrating multiple specialized agents working together. Together, these pieces let an LLM go from "answer one question" to "complete a multi-step task autonomously."

prompt: If I wanted to build my own AI agent today, what would the complete architecture look like? Explain frontend, backend, vector databases, memory, orchestration and deployment.
response: Architecture: Frontend (chat UI, e.g., React/Streamlit) sends user input to your Backend (API server, e.g., FastAPI), which handles auth and routes requests to your Orchestration layer (LangChain/LangGraph) coordinating the LLM calls, tool use, and decision flow. A Vector Database (Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma) stores embeddings for retrieval-augmented context. Memory (short-term in the conversation, long-term in the vector DB or a separate store) keeps context across sessions. Deployment typically means containerizing the backend (Docker), hosting on a cloud service (AWS/GCP/Render), and exposing it via an API the frontend calls.

prompt: Teach me Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) from scratch. Explain embeddings, chunking, vector databases, retrieval, reranking and when RAG should or shouldn't be used.
response: RAG lets an LLM answer using external knowledge instead of relying only on what it memorized during training. Chunking splits documents into manageable pieces. Embeddings convert each chunk into a numeric vector representing its meaning. A vector database stores these vectors for fast similarity search. Retrieval finds the chunks most relevant to a query by comparing embedding similarity. Reranking (optional) uses a more precise model to re-order retrieved chunks by true relevance before feeding them to the LLM. Use RAG when you need up-to-date, private, or large-volume knowledge the model wasn't trained on; skip it for tasks needing pure reasoning/creativity with no external facts needed, since it adds latency and complexity.

prompt: How do Large Language Models actually work internally? Explain transformers, attention, tokenization, embeddings, inference and fine-tuning like you're teaching an engineering student.
response: Tokenization splits text into smaller units (tokens) the model understands. Embeddings convert tokens into numeric vectors capturing meaning. The Transformer architecture processes these vectors through layers using Attention - a mechanism that lets each token "look at" other tokens in the sequence and weigh how relevant they are to understanding context (this is what lets models handle long-range dependencies). Inference is the model predicting the next token repeatedly based on all prior context. Fine-tuning takes a pretrained model and further trains it on a narrower dataset to specialize its behavior for a specific task or style.

prompt: Make me a complete AI Engineer interview preparation roadmap for the next three months with weekly goals.
response: Months 1: Weeks 1-2 - ML fundamentals + Python/PyTorch review. Weeks 3-4 - Deep learning architectures (CNN, RNN, Transformers) + implement from scratch once. Month 2: Weeks 5-6 - LLM concepts (attention, fine-tuning, RAG) + build one GenAI project. Weeks 7-8 - System design for ML (model serving, scaling, data pipelines). Month 3: Weeks 9-10 - DSA practice (medium-level, daily). Weeks 11-12 - Mock interviews, resume polish, and behavioral prep. Keep one active project running throughout to reference in interviews.

prompt: I'm preparing for placements, not FAANG. Give me only the DSA topics and questions that actually matter for companies like Visa, UBS, HSBC and Flipkart.
response: Focus on: Arrays/Strings (two pointers, sliding window), Hashing, Basic Recursion/Backtracking, Sorting/Searching (binary search variants), Linked Lists, Stacks/Queues, Trees (BST operations, traversals), and moderate DP (knapsack-style, LCS). These companies typically ask medium-difficulty LeetCode-style questions, not hard graph/advanced DP problems - practicing top 150 "medium" tagged problems on LeetCode covers most of what shows up.

prompt: Teach me Dynamic Programming in the easiest possible sequence starting from recursion and memoization.
response: Step 1: Write a plain recursive solution (e.g., Fibonacci) - understand the overlapping subproblems by noticing repeated calls. Step 2: Add memoization - store results of subproblems in a dictionary/array so you never recompute them (top-down DP). Step 3: Convert to tabulation - build the answer iteratively from the base case upward using an array (bottom-up DP), which avoids recursion overhead. Practice this sequence on Fibonacci, then climbing stairs, then 0/1 knapsack - each builds directly on the previous pattern.

prompt: Explain the Sliding Window pattern so well that I can identify those questions in interviews without memorizing solutions.
response: Sliding window applies when you need to find a subarray/substring meeting some condition (max sum, longest unique substring, etc.) and the constraint changes predictably as you move through the array. Signal words: "contiguous subarray/substring," "longest/shortest window," "at most/exactly K distinct." Approach: maintain two pointers (left, right) defining a window; expand right to grow the window, and shrink from left when the window violates the condition. This avoids the O(n²) brute force of checking every subarray.

prompt: Review my resume like you're an SDE recruiter. Be brutally honest and tell me exactly what should be improved.
response: I'd need you to paste your resume content for a real review - but generally, recruiters flag: vague bullet points without metrics, generic "team player" language, project descriptions that don't explain technical depth or your specific contribution, and unclear formatting. Paste it in and I'll give you specific, honest feedback line by line.

prompt: Explain Spring Boot project architecture used in production applications. Cover controllers, services, repositories, DTOs, entities, exception handling and authentication.
response: Layered architecture: Controllers handle HTTP requests/responses and route to services. Services contain business logic, calling repositories as needed. Repositories (Spring Data JPA) handle database access. Entities map directly to database tables. DTOs (Data Transfer Objects) shape data sent to/from the client, decoupling your API contract from your internal database structure. Exception handling is centralized via @ControllerAdvice to catch and format errors consistently. Authentication typically uses Spring Security with JWT tokens for stateless auth in production APIs.

prompt: Teach me Multi-Tenant SaaS architecture from scratch. Compare database-per-tenant, schema-per-tenant and shared database approaches.
response: Database-per-tenant: each customer gets their own separate database - strongest isolation and easiest compliance, but expensive to scale and maintain at large tenant counts. Schema-per-tenant: one database, separate schema per tenant - good middle ground on isolation vs. cost. Shared database (shared schema with a tenant_id column): cheapest and simplest to scale, but requires careful query-level isolation (every query must filter by tenant_id) and has less strict data separation. Choice depends on tenant count, compliance needs, and budget - startups often start shared and migrate to schema/database isolation as enterprise customers demand it.

prompt: Explain Redis like I'm a beginner. Why do companies use it? Where does it sit in the system architecture?
response: Redis is an in-memory key-value data store - it's extremely fast because it keeps data in RAM instead of on disk. Companies use it for caching (storing frequently accessed data so the main database isn't hit every time), session storage, rate limiting, and real-time features like leaderboards. Architecturally, it sits between your application server and your main database - the app checks Redis first, and only queries the slower main database if the data isn't cached.

prompt: Compare PostgreSQL and MySQL for startups. Which one should I choose and why?
response: PostgreSQL offers stronger support for complex queries, JSON handling, and advanced data types, plus better standards compliance - good if you expect complex data relationships or analytics needs. MySQL is simpler, slightly faster for straightforward read-heavy workloads, and has massive community support/hosting availability. For most modern startups, PostgreSQL is the safer default choice due to its flexibility (especially JSONB support) as requirements evolve, unless you have a specific reason (like existing team expertise) favoring MySQL.

prompt: Explain how Stripe works behind the scenes. Cover payment flow, subscriptions, webhooks, refunds and security.
response: Payment flow: your app collects card details (via Stripe's secure hosted fields, never touching your servers directly), sends a token to Stripe, which processes the charge with the card network/bank and returns success/failure. Subscriptions are managed as recurring billing objects that automatically charge on a schedule. Webhooks let Stripe notify your backend asynchronously about events (payment succeeded, subscription renewed, etc.) so your app can react. Refunds are issued via API calls that reverse a specific charge. Security relies on PCI compliance being handled by Stripe (card data never touches your servers) plus API keys and webhook signature verification to authenticate requests.

prompt: Act like an investor and brutally evaluate my startup idea. Tell me whether it's worth building or not.
response: I'd need you to describe the actual startup idea for a real evaluation - once you share it, I'll assess it on market size, competition, differentiation, and feasibility, and give you an honest verdict rather than generic encouragement.

prompt: Analyze my startup A2S completely. Market size, competitors, monetization, pricing, MVP, customer acquisition, risks and scalability.
response: To give you a real analysis of A2S, I'd need details on what the product actually does, who it's for, and the problem it solves. Share that and I can break down market size, likely competitors, monetization options, a lean MVP scope, and key risks specific to your idea.

prompt: Create a pitch deck outline that would actually impress an investor.
response: Strong pitch deck structure: 1) Problem (specific, painful, real). 2) Solution (your product, briefly). 3) Market size (TAM/SAM/SOM). 4) Product demo/screenshots. 5) Business model (how you make money). 6) Traction (users, revenue, growth - even early signals). 7) Competition/differentiation. 8) Team (why you're the right people). 9) Financials/projections. 10) The ask (funding amount and use of funds). Keep it to 10-12 slides max - investors skim fast.

prompt: Help me decide what features belong in my MVP and what should be postponed.
response: MVP rule of thumb: include only the single core feature that delivers your product's main value proposition, plus the minimum supporting pieces needed to make that usable (auth, basic UI). Postpone: nice-to-have customization, advanced analytics, integrations with third-party tools, and anything "just in case someone wants it." If a feature isn't needed to prove your core hypothesis with real users, it can wait.

prompt: If you were my CTO, create a six-month product development roadmap for my startup.
response: Generic 6-month roadmap: Months 1-2 - build and ship MVP with core feature set, basic auth, minimal viable infra. Months 3-4 - onboard early users, iterate based on feedback, add most-requested feature, set up analytics/monitoring. Months 5-6 - scale infrastructure for growth, add second key feature, formalize CI/CD and testing, plan for next funding/growth phase. Share your actual product and I can tailor this to specific milestones.

prompt: Research the Visa internship hiring process in detail. What rounds are there? What DSA level do they expect? What projects help?
response: Visa's process typically includes: an Online Assessment (OA) with 2 coding questions, medium DSA difficulty, plus possibly aptitude/MCQ sections; followed by 1-2 technical interviews (DSA + CS fundamentals: OOP, DBMS, OS, networks); and sometimes an HR/behavioral round. DSA expectation is solid medium-level LeetCode practice (arrays, strings, trees, DP basics). Projects that help: anything showing full-stack or backend depth, plus any project demonstrating clean code and understanding of scalability, since Visa values production-mindset thinking.

prompt: Explain the Flipkart GRiD internship process in detail and what happens after each round.
response: GRiD typically starts with an online quiz/coding round (aptitude + DSA), followed by case study or hackathon-style rounds depending on the track (software/product/consulting), and finally interviews for shortlisted candidates. Each round filters down the pool, with the final stage often involving presenting solutions to a panel. Strong performance in the coding round and a well-structured case study presentation are usually the biggest differentiators.

prompt: Create a realistic CAT preparation plan while balancing engineering placements.
response: Given limited time, prioritize by CAT section weightage: dedicate consistent daily blocks (even 1-1.5 hrs) split between Quant, Verbal, and DILR rather than long irregular sessions. Use weekday evenings for concept practice and weekends for full mock tests. During placement season, shift more time to placements temporarily but keep at least 30 min/day of CAT practice to retain momentum rather than restarting from scratch later.

prompt: Compare taking a high-paying software job versus pursuing an MBA over the next ten years.
response: A high-paying software job gives immediate compounding experience, savings, and career progression within tech, with lower risk. An MBA (especially from a top school) can open leadership/general management/consulting paths and significantly boost long-term earning ceiling, but costs 1-2 years of opportunity cost plus tuition, and its ROI depends heavily on school tier and your target role. If you want to stay deeply technical, the job path usually wins; if you want to pivot toward leadership, product, or business strategy, an MBA can accelerate that transition.

prompt: Teach me how to write a proper research paper from literature review to publication.
response: 1) Literature review: survey existing work, identify the gap you're addressing. 2) Define a clear research question/hypothesis. 3) Methodology: describe your approach rigorously enough to be reproducible. 4) Results: present data objectively with appropriate visualizations. 5) Discussion: interpret results, acknowledge limitations. 6) Conclusion: summarize contribution and future work. 7) Choose a target venue (journal/conference) matching your paper's scope, format per their guidelines, and go through peer review, revising based on reviewer feedback before final acceptance.

prompt: Build me a cybersecurity roadmap for beginners covering networking, Linux, web security and penetration testing.
response: Month 1: Networking basics (OSI model, TCP/IP, ports, protocols) + Linux fundamentals (command line, permissions, basic scripting). Month 2: Web security fundamentals (OWASP Top 10, how HTTP works, common vulnerabilities like XSS/SQLi). Month 3: Hands-on practice with tools (Burp Suite, Nmap, Wireshark) on legal practice platforms (TryHackMe, HackTheBox). Month 4+: Penetration testing methodology, capture-the-flag challenges, and pursuing a beginner cert (like Security+ or eJPT) to formalize skills.

prompt: Explain Authentication vs Authorization with JWT, OAuth, cookies and sessions using practical examples.
response: Authentication verifies identity (logging in); Authorization determines permissions after that. JWT (JSON Web Token) is a self-contained token holding user info and claims, signed so the server can verify it wasn't tampered with, often used for stateless auth in APIs. OAuth is a protocol for delegated access - like "Sign in with Google," where you authorize an app to access your data without sharing your password. Cookies are small data stored in the browser, often used to store session IDs. Sessions store user state server-side, referenced by a cookie - useful for traditional web apps, while JWT is more common for stateless API-driven apps.

prompt: Teach me System Design from absolute scratch with real-world examples.
response: Start with core concepts: scalability (vertical vs horizontal scaling), load balancing (distributing traffic across servers), caching (Redis for fast repeated reads), databases (SQL vs NoSQL trade-offs), and CDN (serving static content closer to users). Practice by designing familiar systems step by step: a URL shortener (hashing + database + cache), then a chat app (WebSockets + message queues), then a feed system like Twitter (fan-out strategies, denormalization). Each example builds intuition for trade-offs like consistency vs availability.

prompt: Explain Docker and containers from scratch. Why do companies actually use Docker?
response: A container packages an app with everything it needs (code, dependencies, config) into one portable unit that runs identically anywhere. Docker is the tool that builds, ships, and runs these containers. Companies use it because it eliminates "works on my machine" issues, makes deployment consistent across dev/staging/production, allows fast scaling (spin up more containers instantly), and simplifies onboarding (new devs run one command instead of manually installing a dozen dependencies).

prompt: Compare AWS, Azure and Google Cloud for students and startups. Which one should I learn first?
response: AWS has the largest market share and job demand, making it the safest first choice for career purposes. Azure is strong if you're targeting enterprise/.NET-heavy companies. Google Cloud (GCP) excels in data/ML tooling and is often cheaper for startups with generous free credits. For a student starting out, AWS gives the broadest applicability across job listings, but the underlying concepts (compute, storage, networking) transfer easily between all three once learned.

prompt: Help me design an AI-powered Chrome extension that simplifies complicated webpages. Cover architecture, APIs, UX and monetization.
response: Architecture: a content script extracts page text, sends it to a backend API (or directly to an LLM API) for simplification/summarization, and the extension displays the simplified version in an overlay or sidebar. APIs: use an LLM API (OpenAI/Anthropic) for the simplification logic, plus Chrome's extension APIs (content scripts, storage) for the UI integration. UX: keep it one-click activation with a toggle to compare original vs simplified text, and support adjustable reading levels. Monetization: freemium model - limited free simplifications per day, paid tier for unlimited use or advanced features like PDF support.

prompt: Compare Flutter and Streamlit for building startup MVPs.
response: Flutter builds true cross-platform mobile/web/desktop apps with native performance and full UI control - better if your MVP needs a polished, app-store-ready product. Streamlit is a Python-only tool for quickly building data-focused web interfaces (dashboards, ML demos) with minimal frontend code - much faster to prototype but limited for consumer-facing, highly custom UIs. Choose Flutter for a real product MVP; choose Streamlit for a quick internal tool or data/AI demo to validate an idea fast.

prompt: Explain the difference between Kindle publishing and traditional publishing in India for first-time authors.
response: Kindle Direct Publishing (self-publishing): you retain full control and higher royalty percentage (up to 70%), but handle your own editing, cover design, and marketing, and it can be harder to build initial credibility/discoverability. Traditional publishing: a publisher handles editing, distribution, and marketing, and lends credibility, but royalties are much lower (often 8-15%), the process is slower, and acceptance is competitive. For first-time authors wanting speed and control, KDP is more accessible; for wider distribution and credibility, traditional publishing is stronger if you can get accepted.

prompt: Help me identify a book using only the parts of the story I remember.
response: Sure - share whatever details you remember (characters, setting, plot points, approximate time period, genre, even a vague scene) and I'll try to help narrow down the book.

prompt: Explain B12 deficiency completely. What symptoms are common, when is dizziness related and when should someone worry?
response: B12 deficiency common symptoms: fatigue, weakness, pale skin, tingling/numbness in hands and feet, difficulty concentrating, and mood changes. Dizziness can occur because B12 deficiency can cause mild anemia (reduced oxygen delivery) or affect nerve function related to balance. Worry and seek medical evaluation if dizziness is frequent, accompanied by numbness, memory issues, or persists despite dietary changes - a simple blood test can confirm B12 levels, and this is not a substitute for professional medical advice.

prompt: Analyze this cricketer beyond statistics. Explain technique, strengths, weaknesses, captaincy, fitness and future potential.
response: I'd need you to tell me which cricketer you're referring to for a specific analysis - once you name them, I can break down their technique, playing style strengths/weaknesses, and career trajectory in detail.

prompt: Prepare a complete market research report for this startup idea including TAM, SAM, SOM, competitors, trends and barriers to entry.
response: I'd need the actual startup idea/product description to build a real TAM/SAM/SOM breakdown and competitor analysis - share the specifics and I'll structure a full market research report around it.

prompt: I want to learn this topic properly. Give me the correct order to study it, projects to build, common mistakes, interview relevance and how long each stage should take.
response: Let me know which specific topic you want the learning roadmap for, and I'll lay out the study order, project ideas, common pitfalls, and realistic timelines tailored to it.

prompt: Act as an experienced startup founder and tell me everything that's wrong with my business model before I spend months building it.
response: Share your business model details (what you're selling, to whom, how you make money, and your assumptions about demand) and I'll give you a direct, critical breakdown of the weak points before you commit real time to building.

prompt: If you had to mentor me from scratch for software engineering placements, what would the next 90 days look like?
response: Days 1-30: DSA fundamentals daily (arrays, strings, linked lists, trees, basic DP) + review CS fundamentals (OOP, DBMS, OS basics). Days 31-60: Medium-level DSA practice + one solid full-stack or backend project + system design basics. Days 61-90: Mock interviews, resume refinement, company-specific prep, and daily contest/timed practice to build speed under pressure.

prompt: Compare these two technologies in terms of scalability, learning curve, community support, industry adoption and future scope.
response: Tell me which two technologies you'd like compared and I'll break down scalability, learning curve, community support, adoption, and future outlook for each.

prompt: Explain this research paper like I'm an engineering student. Break down every section into simple language.
response: Share the paper (title, abstract, or full text) and I'll break it down section by section in plain language.

prompt: Create a step-by-step implementation plan for this project from idea to deployment.
response: Share the project idea and I'll lay out a concrete step-by-step plan covering design, build, testing, and deployment phases.

prompt: Tell me the prerequisites I should learn before starting this technology so I don't waste time.
response: Let me know which technology you're planning to learn and I'll list the specific prerequisites so you don't hit unnecessary roadblocks.

prompt: Assume you are a hiring manager. Based on my skills, what gaps would stop you from shortlisting me?
response: Share your current skills/experience (or resume) and I'll give you an honest, hiring-manager-style assessment of gaps that could hold back a shortlist.

prompt: If this startup were presented on Shark Tank or to a VC, what questions would they ask and how should I answer them?
response: Common VC/Shark Tank questions: "What's your traction/revenue so far?", "Why now - why hasn't this been solved already?", "What's your customer acquisition cost vs lifetime value?", "Who's your competition and why will you win?", "What do you need the money for exactly?" Share your specific startup idea and I'll help you prep tailored answers.

prompt: Research the latest industry trends in this field and tell me where the biggest opportunities are over the next five years.
response: Let me know which specific field/industry you want trends and opportunities for, and I'll break down where the momentum is heading.

prompt: Explain this concept visually and intuitively instead of giving textbook definitions.
response: Tell me the specific concept and I'll explain it using an intuitive analogy or visual mental model instead of a formal definition.

prompt: Create the ultimate beginner-to-advanced roadmap for this subject with free resources, projects and milestones.
response: Let me know the subject and I'll build out a beginner-to-advanced roadmap with free resources, hands-on projects, and clear milestones.

prompt: I'm confused between multiple career paths. Compare them based on salary growth, work-life balance, learning opportunities, long-term stability and future demand.
response: Share the specific career paths you're weighing (e.g., SDE vs Data Science vs Product Management) and I'll compare them across salary trajectory, work-life balance, growth, stability, and future demand.

prompt: I have this startup idea. Don't just appreciate it. Tear it apart, tell me every loophole, every risk, every competitor and whether it can actually become a unicorn.
response: Share the actual idea and I'll give it a genuinely critical, no-flattery breakdown of risks, competitors, and realistic scale potential.

prompt: Assume you're an investor from Sequoia or Y Combinator. Ask me every question you would before investing.
response: Sequoia/YC-style questions: "What problem are you solving and for whom, specifically?" "Why are you the right team to build this?" "What's your traction so far?" "How big can this get - is this a billion-dollar market?" "What's your unfair advantage / moat?" "What's your burn rate and runway?" Share your idea and I'll turn this into a real mock Q&A.

prompt: I don't want generic market research. I want actual numbers, TAM, SAM, SOM, CAGR, competitors, revenue models and future trends.
response: Share your specific product/industry and target market, and I'll walk through how to estimate TAM/SAM/SOM with real methodology and where to find CAGR/competitor data for that specific space (note: I can outline the framework and reasoning but can't pull live current-web numbers without browsing access).

prompt: Compare my startup idea with every similar company in India and abroad. Tell me what is unique.
response: Share your startup idea/description and I'll help map out likely comparable companies (India and global) and pinpoint what differentiates your approach.

prompt: If someone had ₹25 lakh today, how should they build this startup from scratch?
response: Share the specific startup idea and I'll break down how to allocate ₹25 lakh realistically - typically split across MVP development, a small founding team/contractors, minimal marketing to validate demand, and a runway buffer, prioritized based on what needs proving first.

prompt: Design the complete tech stack for this startup as if Google engineers were building it.
response: Share what the startup does and its expected scale, and I'll design a tech stack (frontend, backend, database, infra) matched to production-grade, scalable engineering practices.

prompt: Give me every AI feature that can be added to this product in the next five years.
response: Share what the product currently does and I'll brainstorm realistic AI feature additions - personalization, predictive analytics, natural language interfaces, automation of manual workflows, and anomaly detection are common high-value additions depending on the domain.

prompt: Suppose OpenAI built my startup instead of me. What would they do differently?
response: Share your startup idea and I'll reason through what a well-resourced team might prioritize differently - typically things like faster iteration cycles, stronger initial model/data quality, and more aggressive distribution strategy.

prompt: Tell me why this startup could fail before even launching.
response: Share the startup idea and I'll give you a candid pre-mortem of the most likely failure points - market fit, execution risk, competition, or unit economics.

prompt: Research whether customers actually need this or if I'm solving a fake problem.
response: Share the problem/product you're targeting and I'll help you think through validation signals - existing workarounds people use, willingness to pay indicators, and how to test demand cheaply before building.

prompt: Find industries where AI hasn't disrupted much yet.
response: Industries with relatively less AI disruption so far: skilled trades/construction, elder care/caregiving, agriculture (beyond precision farming pilots), local government services, and traditional manufacturing SMEs - largely due to physical-world complexity, regulation, or fragmented small-scale operators making adoption slower.

prompt: What billion-dollar startups can still be built in India using AI?
response: Promising large-scale opportunities in India: AI-powered vernacular language services (education, legal, healthcare access for non-English speakers), AI for agricultural yield optimization at scale, AI-driven SME/MSME financial infrastructure, and AI-native healthcare diagnostics for underserved regions - all address large underserved populations with real willingness to pay once trust is built.

prompt: Give me startup ideas that are difficult to copy.
response: Hard-to-copy startups typically have: proprietary data that improves with usage (network effects), deep domain/regulatory expertise as a moat (healthcare, fintech compliance), strong brand/community lock-in, or unique hardware+software integration. Pure software feature ideas are easiest to copy; defensibility usually comes from data, relationships, or regulation, not the initial feature set.

prompt: Research business models that make recurring revenue instead of one-time income.
response: Recurring revenue models: SaaS subscriptions, usage-based/metered billing, membership models, marketplace take-rates on repeat transactions, and freemium-to-premium conversion. The common thread is delivering ongoing value (not a one-time deliverable) so customers keep paying rather than making a single purchase decision.

prompt: I want industries that will grow for the next 20 years.
response: Long-term growth industries: healthcare/aging population services, renewable energy and climate tech, AI infrastructure and applied AI across sectors, cybersecurity, and education technology - all driven by demographic, environmental, or technological shifts that aren't reversing anytime soon.

prompt: How do companies like Airbnb, Uber and Zillow actually became successful? Break everything down.
response: All three succeeded by solving a two-sided marketplace trust/liquidity problem: Airbnb built trust in staying at strangers' homes via reviews and verified profiles; Uber solved real-time supply-demand matching for rides with GPS and dynamic pricing; Zillow aggregated fragmented real estate data into one accessible, free platform. Common threads: they targeted large existing markets, removed a specific friction point better than incumbents, and used aggressive early growth tactics (Airbnb's Craigslist integration, Uber's city-by-city launches) to bootstrap network effects.

prompt: Give me hidden problems in the real estate industry that nobody talks about.
response: Lesser-discussed real estate problems: opaque pricing and information asymmetry favoring brokers over buyers, extremely fragmented and outdated data across regions, slow/manual paperwork-heavy transaction processes, high broker commission structures that don't scale with effort, and poor post-purchase property management tooling for landlords.

prompt: Research the future of interior design and AI.
response: AI is moving interior design toward: instant photorealistic room visualization from text/photo prompts, automated furniture/product recommendation matched to budget and style, AI-assisted space planning (optimal layouts from room dimensions), and generative tools letting non-designers iterate on styles quickly - shifting professional designers toward curation and execution rather than pure ideation.

prompt: What technologies will replace current real estate platforms?
response: Likely shifts: AI-driven personalized matching (beyond simple filters) between buyers and listings, virtual/AR property tours replacing physical visits for initial screening, blockchain-based property records for faster/more transparent transactions, and predictive pricing models replacing static comparables - though full replacement of current platforms is more likely to be gradual feature evolution than a sudden overhaul.

prompt: I want to know if my startup can become global instead of staying India-focused.
response: Share your product/market and I'll help assess global potential - key factors are whether the problem you solve is universal (vs India-specific regulation/culture-dependent), your unit economics at scale, and whether localization (language, payments, compliance) is feasible for your team size.

prompt: Pretend you're McKinsey. Give me a complete industry report.
response: Share the specific industry you want analyzed and I'll structure a report covering market size, key players, trends, growth drivers, and strategic recommendations in a consulting-style format.

prompt: Pretend you're BCG. Find every weakness in my business.
response: Share your business details (model, market, current metrics) and I'll give a structured, critical breakdown of weaknesses across strategy, operations, and market positioning.

prompt: How would Amazon solve this problem?
response: Share the specific problem and I'll reason through how Amazon's typical approach (customer obsession, data-driven iteration, operational efficiency at scale) might apply to it.

prompt: How would Apple design this product?
response: Share the specific product and I'll reason through it using Apple's typical design philosophy - radical simplicity, tight hardware-software integration, and obsessive focus on user experience over feature count.

prompt: Tell me whether an MBA from IIM Ahmedabad is actually worth it compared to staying in tech.
response: An IIM-A MBA can be worth it if you're aiming for leadership, consulting, PE/VC, or a career pivot away from pure tech execution - the brand and network offer strong ROI for those paths, despite ~2 years of opportunity cost. If you want to stay deeply technical and are already progressing well in tech, the direct compounding of experience and specialization often outpaces the MBA route for pure earning potential in engineering-heavy careers.

prompt: Research career paths where AI and MBA together have the highest salaries.
response: High-paying AI + MBA combination paths: AI Product Management, AI Strategy/Consulting (McKinsey/BCG AI practices), Chief AI Officer / AI transformation leadership roles, and AI-focused venture capital/investing roles - these combine technical fluency with business strategy, commanding premium compensation at senior levels.

prompt: Compare Applied Scientist, ML Engineer, Data Scientist and SDE in terms of future growth.
response: Applied Scientist: research-leaning, high ceiling, requires strong theoretical depth, growing demand as companies operationalize AI research. ML Engineer: production-focused, strong and growing demand as more companies deploy ML at scale. Data Scientist: broad role, demand is shifting toward more specialized ML/analytics engineering as generic "data science" gets automated by tools. SDE: largest overall job pool, stable demand, but AI-assisted coding is changing the skill mix expected. Applied Scientist and ML Engineer currently show the strongest upward trajectory as AI adoption accelerates.

prompt: What skills will still matter after AI automates programming?
response: Skills that remain valuable: system design and architecture judgment, understanding trade-offs and business context (knowing what to build, not just how), debugging complex/ambiguous production issues, security and reliability thinking, and effectively directing/verifying AI-generated code. Pure syntax-level coding becomes less differentiating; judgment and system-level reasoning become more valuable.

prompt: Give me a roadmap to become an Applied Scientist.
response: 1) Strong foundations: linear algebra, probability/statistics, and core ML theory. 2) Deep learning specialization (papers + implementation, not just tutorials). 3) Publish or replicate research papers to build genuine research skill. 4) Build a portfolio of applied projects showing you can take research into working systems. 5) Target research internships or applied science teams to gain real experience, since this role sits between pure research and engineering.

prompt: Research companies that are investing the most in Generative AI.
response: Companies investing most heavily in Generative AI include Microsoft (via OpenAI partnership and Copilot integration), Google (Gemini, DeepMind), Amazon (Bedrock, AWS AI infra), Meta (open-weight Llama models), and NVIDIA (AI infrastructure/chips underlying most GenAI training) - alongside major enterprise adoption spending across nearly every large tech company integrating GenAI into products.

prompt: Tell me which AI startups are most likely to become the next OpenAI.
response: This is inherently speculative, but frontier AI lab contenders often mentioned include Anthropic, xAI, and Mistral, given their focus on foundation model research at scale - though "the next OpenAI" depends heavily on funding access, talent retention, and compute availability, which are hard to predict with confidence.

prompt: I want papers, research directions and unsolved problems in AI.
response: Active unsolved areas: reliable long-horizon agentic reasoning, reducing hallucination/improving factual grounding, efficient training/inference at lower compute cost, better interpretability of model internals, and robust alignment/safety at scale. For papers, searching arXiv's cs.LG and cs.CL categories for recent survey papers on these topics is a strong starting point.

prompt: Explain this research paper like I'm a college student but don't skip technical details.
response: Share the paper and I'll walk through it in accessible language while keeping the technical substance intact.

prompt: What projects should I build if I want recruiters to think I'm exceptional?
response: Recruiters are impressed by projects that show: real-world problem-solving (not tutorial clones), end-to-end ownership (built, deployed, and maintained - not just a notebook), measurable impact or scale considerations, and clean, well-documented code with a live demo. One deep, well-executed project with a clear story beats five shallow ones.

prompt: Research how founders got into Y Combinator without having funding.
response: Founders without prior funding typically get into YC by demonstrating strong traction relative to their stage (even small but growing user numbers), a compelling founder-market fit story, a working MVP showing execution speed, and a clear, sharp articulation of the problem and why now. YC weighs founder capability and evidence of momentum heavily, not prior funding.

prompt: Give me every possible monetization strategy for this product.
response: Share what the product does and I'll map out relevant monetization options - subscription, freemium, usage-based/metered, one-time purchase, marketplace commission, ads, or licensing/B2B - matched to your specific use case.

prompt: Find startup incubators in India that actually help early founders.
response: Well-regarded India-based incubators/accelerators include Y Combinator (India-focused batches), Sequoia Surge, 100X.VC, T-Hub, NASSCOM 10000 Startups, and IIM/IIT-affiliated incubation cells - many offering mentorship, small check funding, and network access rather than just office space.

prompt: Research how companies price SaaS products.
response: Common SaaS pricing models: per-seat/per-user pricing, usage-based/metered pricing, tiered feature-based plans (Basic/Pro/Enterprise), and flat-rate pricing. Companies typically start with simple tiered pricing to ease adoption, then introduce usage-based components as customers scale, and reserve custom/enterprise pricing for large accounts with negotiated terms.

prompt: If I had to pitch this to investors in five minutes, what questions would destroy me?
response: Share your specific pitch/idea and I'll stress-test it with the toughest likely investor questions so you can prepare solid answers in advance.

prompt: Give me a competitive analysis with strengths, weaknesses, opportunities and threats for my startup.
response: Share your startup details and target market, and I'll build out a proper SWOT analysis specific to your positioning and competitors.
